THE REPORT AND RECOMMENDATION OF THE JUDGE PRO TEM IS ADOPTED. SUPPLEMENTAL RESPONSES AND RESPONSIVE DOCUMENTS SHALL BE PROVIDED BY JANUARY 22, 2021. BOTH COUNSEL ARE DIRECTED TO COOPERATIVELY WORK TOWARDS DRAFTING A STIPULATED PROTECTIVE ORDER FOR THE COURT'S SIGNATURE. (SEE PRAKASHPALAN V. ENGSTROM, LIPSCOMB & LACK (2014) 223 CAL.APP.4TH 1105, 1126 ["[W]ITH RESPECT TO AN ATTORNEY'S DUTIES OF CONFIDENTIALITY OWED TO MULTIPLE CLIENTS IN AN AGGREGATE SETTLEMENT, TO THE EXTENT THAT OTHER CLIENTS' SETTLEMENT INFORMATION IS CONFIDENTIAL, THAT DOES NOT PRECLUDE DISCLOSURE OF SOME INFORMATION REGARDING THE SETTLEMENT SUFFICIENT TO PERMIT THE AGGREGATE SETTLEMENT PLAINTIFFS TO DETERMINE WHETHER THE SETTLEMENT FUNDS HAVE BEEN PROPERLY DISTRIBUTED."].) DEFENDANTS' REQUEST FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS IS DENIED. TO THE EXTENT THERE ARE ADDITIONAL RESPONSIVE DOCUMENTS, THEY ARE TO BE PRODUCED. WRITTEN ORDER IS SIGNED. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN; CLERK: J. Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions Against George Wolff, Counsel For Olivier Aufret And Virginie Leclair Pursuant To C.C.P. Sections 128.5 And 128.7; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Nov-12-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANTS LUSCUTOFF, LENDORMY & ASSOCIATES and JEAN-YVES LENDORMY'S Motion For Sanctions Against George Wolff, Counsel For Olivier Aufret And Virginie Leclair Pursuant To C.C.P. Sections 128.5 And 128.7.

The October 13, 2021 motion for sanctions against attorney George Wolfe is denied. While Wolfe's actions were on the border of being frivolous, they did not cross the line into that territory. No attorney fees or costs are awarded to anyone regarding this motion. As I have observed previously, it is time this case was sent out for trial.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard Ulmer, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)

Sidney A. Luscutoff, Esq. (415.989.7500; sidney.luscutoff@lla-law.com) of Luscutoff, Lendormy & Associates for Plaintiffs.
George Wolff, Esq. (415.788.1881 x.222; george@wolfflaw.com) for Defendants.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiff'S Motion To Strike Answer To First Amended Complaint:

Plaintiffs' motion to strike defendants' amended answer to plaintiffs' first amended complaint is denied.

CRC 2.112(3&4) [Memo. 7:1-28] Fairly read, the amended answer states that both defendants deny plaintiffs' allegations and both assert the affirmative defenses as to both plaintiffs.

CCP 431.30(c) [Memo. 8:1-10:17] Defendants emphatically here disclaim any "relief beyond a set-off." (Opp. 3:12-13.) The amended answer adequately pleads an affirmative defense of set-off. The trial court may ignore surplus language. (See CCP 436.)

CCP 436 [Memo. 10:18-13:13] This section of plaintiffs' motion is in effect demurrers; that is not proper in a motion to strike. (5 Witkin, California Procedure (5th ed. 2008) Pleading, sec.1008.) In any event, the trial court may ignore surplus language. (See CCP 436.)

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312

Judge: Richard Ulmer, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)

Off Calendar Sep-24-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. SIDNEY LUSCUTOFF, Esq. of Luscutoff, Lendormy & Associates (Tel: 415-989-7500) for plaintiffs;
Atty. GEORGE W. WOLFF, Esq. of Law Office of George W. Wolff (Tel: 415-788-1881) for defendants/cross-complainants Olivier Auffret and Virginie LeClair;
Atty. KEVIN D. CARDONA, Esq. of Murphy, Pearson, Bradley & Feeney (Tel: 415-788-1900) for cross-defendant Luscutoff, Lendormy & Associates and Jean-Yves Lendormy.

HEARING RE: CROSS-COMPLAINANTS OLIVIER AUFFRET and VIRGINIE LECLAIR's Motion For Order Requiring The Filing Of Amendment To First Amended Cross-Complaint.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The "motion for order requiring the filing of amendment to first-amended cross-complaint [FACC] of defendants/cross-complainants Olivier Auffret and Virginie LeClair" is denied. On May 7, 2021, summary judgment was granted on the FACC on statute-of-limitations grounds. It is argued that the court has "inherent authority" to "reconsider that order of summary judgment," but the time for reconsideration has long passed. (CCP 1008.) In any event, reconsideration would not change my rulings. Even an improvidently amended FACC would still be time-barred.

On September 16, 2021, the day the above tentative ruling was initially posted, defendants/cross-complainants filed a "motion for peremptory challenge" under CCP 170.6. That motion is denied. A CCP 170.6 peremptory challenge cannot be entertained as to subsequent hearings that are a part of or a continuation of the original proceedings. (Jacobs v. Sup. Ct. (1959) 53 Cal.2d 187, 192; see also Pickett v. Sup. Ct. (2012) 203 Cal.App.4th 887, 893; CCP 1008 (motion for reconsideration must be made to "same judge"); In re Marriage of Oliverez (2015) 238 Cal.App.4th 1242, 1247 (reconsideration by second judge "only appropriate when the prior judge is unavailable").)

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/RBU)

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiffs' motion "for leave to file their first amended complaint" is granted. Denial of such a motion is rarely justified. (Morgan v. Sup. Ct. (1959) 172 Cal.App.2d 527, 530.) The proposed amended complaint's validity is not considered when deciding leave to amend. (Kittredge Sports Co. v. Sup. Ct. (1989) 213 Cal.App.3d 1045, 1048.)

Order signed.

Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike First Amended Cross-Complaint Of Olivier Auffret And Virgine Leclair
Off Calendar Feb-10-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Kevin Cardona, Esq. 415-788-1900 of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney for Plaintiff/Cross Defendant LUSCUTOFF, LENDORMY & ASSOCIATES, JEAN-YVES LENDORMY.
George Wolff, Esq. 415-788-1881 x2363 for Defendant Olivier Auffret.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court announces its revised tentative ruling to grant Defendant's motions for sanctions in the amount of $6,927.50 per the applicable provision for reasons stated on the record. Plaintiff argues. Defendant responds. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT LUSCUTOFF, LENDORMY & ASSOCIATES, JEAN-YVES LENDORMY'S Motion To Strike First Amended Cross-Complaint Of Olivier Auffret And Virgine Leclair.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Cross-defendants Luscutoff, Lendormy & Associates and Jean-Yves Lendormy's motion to strike amended cross-complaint is denied. First, the motion is moot because attorney Wolff filed a second amended verification that supersedes the prior one. Second, Code of Civil Procedure, 446 expressly allows for attorney verification in the instant circumstances because the clients reside in France. "The object of a verification is to assure good faith in the averments or statements of a party." (Frio v. Superior Crt. (1988) 203 Cal.App.3d 1480, 1498.) Generally, where there is no allegation of bad faith, a claim of inadequate verification is without merit. (See League of Women Voters v. Eu (1992) 7 Cal.App.4th 649, 656.) "The defect [improper verification] is one of form which does not affect the substance." (See Soltani-Rastegar v. Sup. Crt. (1989) 208 Cal.App.3d 424, 428.) DeCamp v. First Kensington Corp. (1978) 83 Cal.App.3d 268 is distinguishable. In that case, there was evidence of bad faith.

In addition, Cross-Defendant has 20 days to answer. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate Default And Default Judgment Of Or As To Defendants Olivier Auffret And Virginie Leclair
Off Calendar Oct-30-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT OLIVIER AUFFRET, VIRGINIE LECLAIR'S Motion To Vacate Defaults And Default Judgment Of Or As To Defendants Olivier Auffret And Virginie Leclair.

Defendants' motion to vacate the default and default judgment taken against them on August 27 and September 6, 2019 is granted. A defendant may seek discretionary relief from default on grounds of "mistake, inadvertence, surprise or excusable neglect." (Code Civ. Proc., 473(b).) "Section 473 is often applied liberally where the party in default moves promptly to seek relief, and the party opposing the motion will not suffer prejudice if relief is granted." (Elston v. City of Turlock (1985) 38 Cal.3d 227, 233.) Defendants set forth facts showing excusable neglect, entitling them to relief.

First, "[t]o entitle a party to relief [for excusable neglect] the acts which brought about the default must have been the acts of a reasonably prudent person under the same circumstances." (Jackson v. Bank of America (1983) 141 Cal.App.3d 55, 58.) Defendants, French residents and citizens, show that following service, they were reasonably diligent in contacting their French counsel and seeking to retain an attorney in the US, that upon receiving a retainer agreement they promptly signed it and sent it back, and that it took some time to pay counsel's fees because of banking and holiday delays. (Auffret/LeClair Decl. 3-19.)

Second, an application for discretionary relief shall be made within a reasonable time, in no case exceeding six months." (Code Civ. Proc. 473(b).) This motion was made within a reasonable time. Defendants filed this motion on October 7, about a month after default judgment was entered.

Third, a motion under 473 is granted liberally if the party opposing the motion will not suffer prejudice if relief is granted. (Elston, 38 Cal.3d at 233.) Plaintiffs' argument that if relief is granted they would be "required to spend much time and incur large expenses to defend themselves" is insufficient to show prejudice. (See Plfs.' Opp. to Mot. to Vacate at 5; Rogalski v. Nabers Cadillac (1992) 11 Cal.App.4th 816, 822 ["The only true prejudice to [plaintiff] is that he will now have to go back and try the case on the merits. But since the law strongly favors trial and disposition on the merits, there is no prejudice."].)

Finally, Defendants sufficiently support their motion for relief. "Case law . . . requires the party seeking relief to accompany his notice of motion for relief with an affidavit of merits or verified pleading which sets up a valid defense." (Transit Ads v. Tanner Motor Livery (1969) 270 Cal.App.2d 275, 281.) Defendants include with their motion a general denial with several defenses to the complaint, and thus meet their requirements for relief under CCP 473(b). (Defs.' RJN ISO Mot. to Vacate, Ex. 12.)

Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

Motion Of Plaintiffs Luscutoff, Lendormy And Associates And Jean-Yves Lendormy For Order Directing The Filing Of An Undertaking [Ccp Sec. 1030]
Off Calendar Oct-30-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF LUSCUTOFF, LENDORMY & ASSOCIATES, JEAN-YVES LENDORMY's Motion For Order Directing The Filing Of An Undertaking [Ccp Sec. 1030]

Plaintiffs' motion for an order directing the filing of an undertaking pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 1030 is denied. Section 1030 provides: "When the plaintiff in an action or special proceeding resides out of the state, or is a foreign corporation, the defendant may at any time apply to the court by noticed motion for an order requiring the plaintiff to file an undertaking to secure an award of costs and attorney's fees." Plaintiffs argue under Code of Civil Procedure 387, a plaintiff includes a cross-complainant. Plaintiffs are wrong. Yao v. Superior Court (2002) 104 Cal.App.4th 327 squarely rejected this exact argument, holding that a California plaintiff who sues an out-of-state defendant cannot move the court to require the defendant/cross-complainant to post security in order to prosecute the cross-complaint, for two reasons: "The first is that section 1030 unambiguously refers only to a 'plaintiff'; when the Legislature intends for a specific statutory provision to apply to both a plaintiff and cross-complainant, it expressly indicates that intent. The second is that to construe the statute to include an out-of-state cross-complainant would not promote the policy served by the statute." 'that "plaintiff could not move the trial court to require defendant to post security under Code Civ. Proc., 1030, in order to prosecute the cross-complaint. Section 1030 unambiguously refers only to a 'plaintiff,' and when the Legislature intends for a specific statutory provision to apply to both a plaintiff and a cross-complainant, it expressly indicates that intent." (Id. at 331-332.) Contrary to Plaintiffs' unsupported argument on reply, Yao is binding on this Court. (Auto Equity Sales, Inc. v. Superior Court (1962) 57 Cal.2d 450, 455.)

Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)
